name: Alvin Stoller
abstract: Alvin Stoller (October 7, 1925 – October 19, 1992) was an American jazz drummer. He was best known for playing drums on both Mitch Miller's recording of "The Yellow Rose of Texas" and Stan Freberg's parody of Miller's recording.

concerts.label: Carnegie Pop Concert: Jazz at the Philharmonic
Carnegie Pop Concert: Jazz at the Philharmonic

about_quotes.speaker: Abe Most
about_quotes.evidence: When I left Tommy this octet, which had Alvin Stoller and people like that in it, Paul Smith, very good people in that octet.
about_quotes.doc_title: Abe Most interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 13, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions being in a jam session with Tommy Dorsey, Ray Bauduc, etc., but Alvin Stoller is only mentioned by the interviewer as appearing in the film with the Dorsey orchestras; the interviewee does not mention interacting with or knowing Stoller personally.

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: The other record was a 45 rpm on Imperial records and has Oscar still playing cello and features Arnold Ross, piano, Harry Babasin, cello, Joe Comfort, bass and Alvin Stoller drums.

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: The first day Alvin Stoller played drums. He played two days, I think. And the next day or day and a half, Jess'e Price played, till we finished the date.

about_quotes.speaker: Red Callender
about_quotes.evidence: Alvin Stoller has a newspaper clipping he took out that shows a Petrillo quote
